comments.text: One usually guesses that the prime number theorem in short intervals holds for intervals of power size, i.e. we have $\pi(x+y) - \pi(x) = (1+o(1)) y/\log x$ as long as $ x^{\delta}\leq y < x$ for any $\delta>0$. That implies a negative answer to this problem: The lim inf is positive if and only if there exists $\epsilon>0$ and $M$ such that for any $x>M$ we have $\frac{ |A \cap [1,x]|}{\pi(x)}> \epsilon$. Then choose $\delta<\epsilon$ and find $n$ arbitrarily large with $n-a$ prime for all $a\in A$ with $0<a<n$. Let $x= \lceil n^\delta \rceil$ and then we have $\pi(n) - \pi(n-x) \geq |A \cap [1,x]| > \epsilon \pi (x) =(1+o(1)) \epsilon x/\log x = (1+o(1)) \epsilon x/ (\delta \log n) > (1+o(1)) x/\log n$ contradicting the prime number theorem in short intervals. However, we are very far from being able to prove the prime number theorem in short intervals, and turning this intuition into a rigorous proof seems difficult.

Given that $\epsilon/\delta>1$, I guess you meant "$>(1+o(1))x/\log n$" instead. Clearly, combining your argument with the latest admissible values $\delta>17/30$ due to Guth and Maynard gives\[\liminf_{x\to\infty}\frac{|A\cap[1,x]|}{\pi(x)}\le \frac{17}{30},\]provided such an $A$ exists.

The weak version of the prime $k$-tuple conjecture known as Dickson's conjecture suffices for a proof that there exists a set $A\subseteq\mathbb N$ such that for infinitely many $n$, $n-a$ is prime for every $a\in A\cap[1,n)$ and\[\limsup_{x\to\infty}\frac{|A\cap[1,x]|}{\pi(x)}\ge1.\]A simple but useful observation is that if $\mathcal{H}$ is a set of integers such that for some integer $n$, $|n-h|$ is a prime $>|\mathcal{H}|$ for every $h\in \mathcal{H}$, then $\mathcal{H}$ is also admissible. Indeed, if $\mathcal{H}\pmod{p}=\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ for some prime $p\le|\mathcal{H}|$, then $p\mid n-h$ for some $h\in\mathcal{H}$. Since $|n-h|>|\mathcal{H}|$ is prime, we have $p=|n-h|>|\mathcal{H}|$, a contradiction. Assume Dickson's conjecture that for any admissible set $\mathcal{H}=\{h_1,...,h_k\}$, the linear forms $n+h_1,...,n+h_k$ take prime values simultaneously infinitely often. We construct a set $A$ with the desired properties inductively as follows. Start with the admissible set $A_1=\{1\}$ and $n_1=2$. Suppose that we have built an admissible set $A_{j-1}$ with $\max A_{j-1}<n_{j-1}$. Dickson's conjecture implies that there exists an integer $n_j\ge n_{j-1}^2$ such that $n_j-a$ is prime for every $a\in A_{j-1}$. Set $A_j=A_{j-1}\cup A_{j-1}'$, where\[A_{j-1}':=\{n_{j-1}<a\le n_{j}/2:n_j-a~\text{is prime}\}.\]Then $n_j-a\ge n_j/2$ is prime for every $a\in A_j$. Since\[|A_j|=|A_{j-1}|+\big|A_{j-1}'\big|<n_{j-1}+\pi(n_j)<n_j/2\]when $n_j$ is sufficiently large. By our observation, $A_j$ is admissible. This allows us to continue the process inductively. Finally, put $A=\bigcup_{j\ge1}A_j$. Then $n_j-a$ is prime for every $a\in A\cap[1,n_j]=A_j$. Moreover, for $x_j=n_j/2$,\[\frac{|A\cap[1,x_j]|}{\pi(x_j)}\ge \frac{\big|A_{j-1}'\big|}{\pi(x_j)}=\frac{\pi(n_j/2-n_{j-1})}{\pi(n_j/2)}=1+o(1)\]for sufficiently large $j$, as desired. On the other hand, if $n-a$ is prime for every $a\in A\cap[1,n]$, then for any $1\le x<n$,\[\{n-a:a\in A\cap[1,x]\}\subseteq(n-x,n)\]consists of primes. So if there are infinitely many such $n$, then\[|A\cap[1,x]|\le \pi(n)-\pi(n-x)\le(1+o(1))\frac{2x}{\log x}\]for sufficiently large $x$ and infinitely many $n>x$. Hence,\[\limsup_{x\to\infty}\frac{|A\cap[1,x]|}{\pi(x)}\le2.\]It would be interesting to know the true value of this $\limsup$. Interestingly, the second Hardy--Littlewood conjecture $\pi(x+y)\le \pi(x)+\pi(y)$, which was shown to be incompatible with the Hardy--Littlewood prime $k$-tuple conjecture by Hensley and Richards, would imply that this $\limsup$ is $\le1$.
